chapters.title: Writing mastery is the art of working with reality
chapters.text:
Craft includes plot design, conflict, characterization, structure, and the lived social world of workshops—yet it never detaches from the era that produces it. A novel set among elves or on a distant planet still encodes the assumptions of its author and audience.

Classical Greek models, especially Aristotle, trained generations to treat one heroic figure as the engine of narrative. Salesses argues that real experience is messier: agency spreads across communities, institutions, and histories that a single protagonist cannot contain.

Writing is a bundle of expectations accumulated from earlier books and present readers.

For centuries a narrow privileged readership—often white, male, and heterosexual—defined what sounded "natural" on the page. Salesses urges writers to rebuild craft for plural stories. A damaging convention treats whiteness as the default race unless stated otherwise; when identity shapes plot, it belongs in craft as explicitly as place and period. Marking race for every character, not only for people of color, can correct that imbalance.

Aristotle wrote within a society that normalized slavery and reserved publication for elites. People do not elect their class or ethnicity, so fiction must stage lives beyond the center. As society shifts, narrative technique must shift with it.

Creativity is always situated. Salesses supplies methods to loosen inherited rules, redesign workshops around author biography, and practice drafting with clearer purpose. The goal is work that feels truthful to both writer and reader rather than merely compliant with old templates.

Workshops that ignore context often polish voice while stripping cultural specificity. Salesses recommends asking whose comfort the revision serves before accepting blanket advice about clarity or pacing.

He also encourages writers to document the social positions of characters with the same care they give to geography. Class, migration history, and language register shape scene dynamics as much as weather or architecture. Ignoring those layers produces fiction that feels universal only because it silently centers one kind of life.

chapters.title: Culture and expectations influence the craft
chapters.text:
We read sentences holistically; individual words vanish when they match cultural habit. Salesses steers students toward plain dialogue tags like "said" and "asked" because ornate alternatives such as "queried" snag the eye. If an entire literary culture adopted "queried," it would become invisible there too—transparency is learned, not innate.

Non-native English writers sometimes overload synonyms in early drafts, believing variety signals mastery. Hemingway repeated familiar tags because readers expected them. Repetition is not failure when it serves comprehension.

Imagine authors from a community that prefers "query" entering a workshop steeped in "ask." Peers may urge them to standardize diction, unintentionally rerouting the book toward a different audience.

If art matters to life, art inherits life's obligations. ~ Matthew Salesses

Craft, then, is the management of shared expectation. Western psychological realism often ends with characters reabsorbed into social order—Elizabeth Bennet marries, Daisy Buchanan returns to Tom—regardless of private happiness. Norms swallow people and plots together. Expectations are not always prisons; Cinderella structures still sell, as Kurt Vonnegut quipped.

Narrative habits also train how we read reality.

Joseph Campbell's monomyth elevates a male hero repeating familiar trials, yet heroines or collective journeys deserve equal footing. Stories need not always follow a lone conqueror through obstacle courses.

When workshops label diction or structure "wrong," they may be measuring deviation from one baseline—not universal craft law.

Native and non-native writers alike benefit from naming the reading community they hope to reach, then testing pages with members of that community instead of only with peers trained on the same canon.

Genre conventions carry cultural baggage too. A romantic comedy arc that demands individual coupling may ignore collectivist values; a war story focused on solitary heroism may erase caretaking labor. Recognizing those defaults lets writers either subvert or satisfy them deliberately rather than accidentally.

Students trained on Western realism sometimes misread deliberate ambiguity as error. Salesses asks them to separate "I would not write it this way" from "this fails on its own terms." That distinction alone can prevent workshops from homogenizing voice.

chapters.title: Writers create for the whole world, but they always have a specific audience
chapters.text:
Every manuscript imagines a listener. By default many writers still pitch toward privileged readers.

Western five-part plotting—setup, rising action, climax, resolution—mirrors one lineage. East Asian kishotenketsu (introduction, development, twist, reconciliation) pursues a different rhythm with equal intentionality. Chinese critic Zheng Zhenduo defends its integrity while some Western reviewers dismiss unfamiliar structures as "formless" when they simply address another public.

Craft is inseparable from identity, culture, and power. ~ Matthew Salesses

Authors should prioritize readers equipped to meet the work on its terms instead of diluting it for everyone. Salesses does not argue that white readers cannot engage African fiction—only that deeper cultural literacy requires time. Translators of One Thousand and One Nights often trim repetition for Western markets even though repetition signals artistry elsewhere.

"Good writing" is always relational.

Some Black writers compose for dual audiences: gatekeepers who control access and community readers who decode hidden narrative keys embedded in the text. Cultural fluency sharpens every sentence.

Implied reader and implied author shape reception. Two accounts of 9/11—one inward, one explanatory—assume different prior knowledge. The inward version trusts shared national memory; the explanatory version teaches outsiders what insiders already feel. Purpose fuses topic with audience.

Before revising, name who must understand on first read and who may need scaffolding. Strategy replaces guesswork.

When editors demand explainers for references that are ordinary within your community, the edit may be widening the audience at the cost of intimacy. Sometimes the right answer is a footnote; sometimes it is a firmer commitment to the implied reader.

Hidden narrative—coded references, inside jokes, untranslated phrases—can honor readers who share the author's background while still inviting outsiders who are willing to learn. The technique is not obscurity for its own sake but respect for multiple literacies within one text.

Translation history offers a cautionary lesson: every cut for "flow" is also a decision about who deserves the full texture of the source. Writers working in English can apply the same awareness by refusing to sand away cadences that sound nonstandard to some ears but home to others.

chapters.title: Tone creates an attitude towards the world, and conflict reflects reality
chapters.text:
Tone broadcasts worldview. Robert Boswell describes it as distance between narrator and character; humor applied to solemn figures can make them appear ridiculous even when they take themselves seriously.

Tone also guides how characters evaluate one another and how readers should judge them. Distinguish enduring tone from passing emotion—a grief novel still requires anger, joy, and surprise along the way.

Tone need not echo the protagonist's mood. A rural teen entering a metropolis might romanticize, resent, or remake the city; narrative attitude shifts as the environment shifts.

Conflict roots fiction in social fact. Authors select racial, economic, and cultural stakes that become the story's pressure system. Classic oppositions—person against person, world, or self—imply distinct models of reality and therefore distinct implied audiences.

Convincing conflict feels observed, not manufactured.

Character arc and plot arc interlock. A poor honest figure who grows wealthy and corrupt demonstrates moral decline while the world rewards compromise; Cinderella's marriage ending argues that belonging demands conformity. Plot and person carry separate moral arguments.

Selecting conflict is therefore ethical: Does the world change? Does the protagonist? Or does the narrative insist that neither can?

Salesses treats these choices as craft decisions with political consequence, not neutral devices.

Revision checklists should include: Does this scene assume a reader who shares my references? Does conflict emerge from social conditions or only from private temperament? Would a different structure honor the story's origin culture?

Boswell's distance metaphor helps writers calibrate irony, sympathy, and critique without confusing them. A narrator who stands far from a character can expose systemic harm; a narrator who stands close can document interior damage. Both are craft choices with ethical weight.

Conflict models that pit one person against the world can erase collective struggle; models that pit person against self can privatize political harm. Salesses wants writers to name which model they chose and what reality it implies about change.

Tone exercises—writing grief in a comic register or joy in a bleak one—reveal how much meaning rides on narrative stance rather than on event selection alone.

Readers feel tone before they articulate theme; practice shifting stance to shift meaning.

chapters.title: The character should be unique, not relatable
chapters.text:
Characterization animates figures through action, speech, and thought, yet relationships reveal essence most clearly.

Credibility means steering material to comment on both the fictional world and the one we inhabit. Setting prompts matter: What formed this character? What do they know? What formed the narrator, and what blind spots remain?

Structure orders meaning. Salesses cites a workshop piece about a Black woman whose brother sells drugs and a well-meaning white officer next door. Opening and closing in her viewpoint centers her interior life; the same events through the officer's lens would preach a different politics. Cyclical forms honored in some cultures carry significance linear Western models overlook.

Readers inevitably compare themselves to characters on the page.

Relatability, however, is not craft—it is prior reading culture reframed as universality. Hobbies should illuminate personality, not pander. When writers make aliens love pizza to comfort a white mainstream audience, they dictate who counts as reader.

America's "invisible" realist mode—sparse metaphor, muted politics—lets characters "angrily eat a potato" instead of debating ideology aloud. Hemingway and later Raymond Carver mainstreamed the style.

Chasing relatability can flatten difference into a mirror for the already centered. Write toward people without predefining who they must be.

Point-of-view decisions are political. Centering the marginalized gaze for opening and closing frames can refuse the trope of tragedy filtered through a savior figure.

Salesses warns against workshop feedback that praises "relatability" when the character simply duplicates dominant-culture hobbies. Specificity—odd passions, regional food, nonstandard family structures—often generates deeper connection than forced sameness.

Structure is never neutral chronology. Flashbacks, circular returns, and choral narration can embody memory practices from cultures where time is experienced communally rather than as a straight line toward individual triumph.

chapters.title: Ideas for new workshops and exercises for writers
chapters.text:
Salesses reframes workshops as spaces where writers declare the expectations they pursue and the risks they still want to take. Useful feedback examines each audience separately and respects author intent.

One writer's revelation may bore another—that is normal.

In one experiment Salesses ran question-only sessions so authors spoke instead of silently absorbing verdicts. Without author voice, manuscripts lose aim.

Models he recommends:

1. Liz Lerman's Critical Response Process: observers respond, the author questions them, participants question the author, then—if invited—offer suggestions.
2. Question-only or suggestion-only formats, alternating who leads.
3. Parallel workshops for short drafts where everyone reads and discusses simultaneously.
4. Structured debate: the author lists craft choices and teams defend or challenge them.

Exercises include:

• Draft a sorrowful scene in a comic tone (or the reverse), then analyze how diction and setting created the dissonance.
• Read aloud to locate dull or false passages.
• Delete a character and observe how theme shifts.
• Replace abstract emotion with concrete sensory detail.

Critique should begin by asking which cultural contract a draft assumes before prescribing universal fixes.

Exercises that pair mismatched tone and emotion teach writers how much attitude shapes meaning independent of plot events.

Debate-style workshops make trade-offs visible: the author states why a nonlinear chapter exists, why dialect appears untranslated, or why an ending refuses closure. Defenders and critics then argue from stated intent rather than unspoken norms.

Peer critique should close with the author restating purpose in their own words: who the piece is for, what tradition it engages, and which risks they refuse to abandon. That closing ritual keeps workshops from becoming conformity machines that reward only familiar forms.

Authors who explain intent last leave workshops with manuscripts still aimed at their chosen public.

Exercises that remove characters or abstract language force writers to see which elements actually carry meaning for their intended readers.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Salesses extends both craft teaching and workshop culture beyond inherited norms. Characters exceed checklists of traits readers want to see in themselves. Stories may unsettle; honesty still matters. Let environments test protagonists until they breathe as individuals with independent voices.

Write for a chosen audience rather than a mythical everyone. Expand technique to include diverse bodies, motives, and structures. Conflict can exceed private self-improvement. Culture always inflects form—interrogate stereotypes by answering: Who reads this? What is it about? What does the protagonist want? Will they or the world change by the final page?

Workshops should be shared imagination, not dominance games that erase culture or silence authors. Writers advance faster through mutual aid than through policing one another's manuscripts.

Try this

• Read page one to friends, record their predictions, rewrite, and compare versions.
• Danzy Senna's prompt: in five minutes list traits with "She/he/they were the type of person who…"—make them surprising.

Craft in the real world means letting form serve truth instead of comforting readers who wish every story resembled those they already know.

Read it till the end?

Salesses ultimately argues that truthful craft is situational ethics: choose form, conflict, and characterization to reflect the world you inhabit, not the workshop that happened to approve your first story.

The concluding invitation is practical: test your opening page with readers who mirror your intended audience, then revise until their expectations align with the story you mean to tell—not the story they assumed from prior books alone.
